explanation: Although the findings of an initial MRI of the brain were described as normal, the symptoms and clinical findings of the neuro-ophthalmologist in conjunction with the findings on review of the MRI raised suspicion for sarcoidosis involving the central nervous system. Neurosarcoidosis can present a diagnostic challenge in indolent, relapsing cases, and the clinician should maintain a high index of suspicion despite a negative workup. Given the rarity of central nervous system involvement in sarcoidosis and the low chance of false-negative results on PET, repeating the testing is essential with disease evolution as well (choice C).1,2 A repeated PET/CT scan showed fluorodeoxyglucose-avid cervical, mediastinal, and hilar lymphadenopathy in a pattern suggestive of sarcoidosis (Figure 2), and a tissue biopsy of a hilar lymph node was characterized by noncaseating granulomas with proliferation of epithelioid cells.A repeated positron emission tomography/computed tomography scan 9 years after initial presentation revealed fluorodeoxyglucose-avid cervical, mediastinal, and hilar lymphadenopathy in a pattern suggestive of sarcoidosis.Neither corticosteroids (choice A) nor other immunomodulator medications would reveal the causative mechanism of this relapsing optic neuropathy and the cranial neuropathies. However, these medications can provide control of symptoms and should be administered to patients with definite neurosarcoidosis.The MRI findings in the central nervous system (choice B) in patients with neurosarcoidosis are nonspecific, overlapping with numerous inflammatory, infectious, and neoplastic processes.3 Given the history of breast carcinoma, clinicians should always include leptomeningeal metastasis in the differential diagnosis. However, that would be less likely, given the multiple responses to steroids, the absence of other symptoms, and the poor prognosis associated with leptomeningeal metastasis. A repeated MRI (9 years after initial presentation) showed new enhancement of the left cranial nerve III in a T1 image, before and after contrast. Primary central nervous system lymphoma was also included in the differential diagnosis. The patient s cerebrospinal fluid was characterized by lymphocytosis of an unclear causative mechanism. Additionally, neither unusual cells in flow cytometry nor malignant cells by cytologic testing were detected.It has been demonstrated that serum angiotensin-converting enzyme levels are elevated only in 30% to 40% of patients with isolated neurosarcoidosis, compared with 60% in those with systemic disease (choice D). In addition, cerebrospinal fluid angiotensin-converting enzyme levels are estimated to be elevated in only 28% of affected individuals.4 Furthermore, while elevated protein and pleocytosis in the cerebrospinal fluid are consistent with neurosarcoidosis, they are not considered pathognomonic.5Targeted immunomodulatory therapy was started after the establishment of the diagnosis. The patient reported feeling better overall. Her neuro-ophthalmic examination results have been stable since then, and she continued to have decreased visual acuity with dyschromatopsia (with greater intensity in the right eye than the left eye), with a relative afferent pupillary defect on the right eye and right-sided hearing loss. The patient has followed up with autoimmune neurology and neuro-ophthalmology.
Question: A 55-year-old white woman presented with 1 week of decreased visual acuity and ache on abduction in the right eye. She had decreased right-sided hearing and tinnitus, a moderate right-sided frontal headache, and an unsteady gait. She did not have diplopia, facial numbness, or paresthesias. She had a history of stage IV breast cancer treated with letrozole after a mastectomy 5 years prior. On an initial examination, her best-corrected visual acuity was 20/50 OD and 20/25 OS, with dyschromatopsia, relative afferent pupillary defect, and visual-field defects on the right side. Her extraocular movements were full. A dilated fundus examination demonstrated a pale right optic nerve. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain was reported to have normal results, but on review, we noted thickening and enhancement of both optic nerves (with the right eye larger than the left eye) (Figure 1A), cranial nerve III in the right eye, cranial nerve V bilaterally, the pituitary infundibulum, and the hypothalamus, as well as adjacent cavernous sinuses. Leptomeningeal thickening over the convexities of the anterior frontal lobes was also evident (Figure 1B). The patient s cerebrospinal fluid contained a small population of polyclonal CD19+ B cells and CD4+ and CD8+ T cells. An extensive laboratory workup, including tests for HIV, antinuclear antibodies, anti double-stranded DNA, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies, serum protein electrophoresis, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein, complete blood cell count, antiganglioside antibodies, anti myelin-associated glycoprotein antibodies, and angiotensin-converting enzyme, gave normal results. A computed tomographic (CT) scan of the thorax and a whole-body positron emission tomography (PET)/CT scan revealed no abnormalities.Clinically, she initially responded to corticosteroids. Over the ensuing 5 years, she had multiple similar presentations that also included facial pain and paresthesias. She also developed a chronic small-fiber peripheral neuropathy and was treated with mycophenolate mofetil, cyclophosphamide, and intravenous immunoglobulin at various points. She remained clinically stable for 4 years and returned with similar symptoms.Repeat the magnetic resonance imaging of the brainRepeat the testing for angiotensin-converting enzyme in serum and cerebrospinal fluid What Would You Do Next?

caption: A, Pustular eruption of the face characterized by monomorphous 1- to 2-mm pustules over background erythema on the cheeks, chin, and nose appearing less than 24 hours after sun exposure. Punch biopsy of skin demonstrated intrafollicular (B and C) and subcorneal (B and D) pustule formation (hematoxylin-eosin).
explanation: Histopathologic examination revealed subcorneal and follicular pustules (Figure, B-D). Periodic acid Schiff and Grocott methenamine silver stain results were negative for fungal organisms. Bacterial swab results from malar lesions were negative for growth. Considering failure of multiple treatments effective for papulopustular rosacea and acne vulgaris and the episodic and photo-aggravated nature of the eruptions, a diagnosis of actinic folliculitis was rendered. Phototesting was not performed owing to patient preference. Despite this, the clinical and histopathologic findings were considered sufficiently diagnostic. The patient initiated treatment with adapalene gel, 0.3%, and reported decreased severity of eruptions with fewer pustules and faster resolution. She was able to prevent recurrences with strict use of a solar umbrella in summer months. The treatment responses further supported the diagnosis.Actinic folliculitis is a monomorphic pustular dermatitis that presents on the face, with possible involvement of the arms and upper chest. The eruption normally occurs within 4 to 14 hours of sunlight exposure and resolves over 7 to 10 days.1,2 This entity encompasses 2 previously separate disorders, acne aestivalis and actinic superficial folliculitis.1,3 Acne aestivalis was previously described as a pruritic, papular eruption of the face induced by sunlight. Actinic superficial folliculitis is characterized by pustules distributed over the shoulders, upper trunk, and arms that typically appear 24 to 36 hours after sun exposure.1,3Current case reports of actinic folliculitis in the literature describe patients between the ages of 17 and 42 years, but analysis regarding the average age at onset is lacking. The pathogenesis of the disease remains undetermined. However, some have hypothesized that epidermal thickening induced by UV radiation causes follicular infundibular occlusion, inflammation, and lesion development.2 Histopathological descriptions in the literature include perifollicular mixed inflammatory infiltrate, perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate, and subcorneal pustulosis.1,3,4The differential diagnosis of actinic folliculitis includes papulopustular rosacea, pinpoint papular light eruption, acute localized exanthematous pustulosis, acne vulgaris, and photoallergy. Papulopustular rosacea presents with papules and pustules overlying erythema in a central facial distribution. Biopsy results are often nonspecific and unnecessary for diagnosis, but characteristic histopathology includes mixed perivascular and perifollicular infiltrates in the superficial and mid-dermis with accumulation of neutrophils beyond the follicle.5 Papulopustular rosacea was considered unlikely given the failed response to established rosacea treatment regimens.Pinpoint papular light eruption is a clinical variant of polymorphous light eruption characterized by pinpoint papules and vesicles 30 minutes to 3 days after UV light exposure, most commonly in dark-skinned individuals.6 Histopathologic findings correspond to clinical course. Acute lesions are similar to those of classical polymorphous light eruption and show spongiosis, edema, and superficial and deep perivascular infiltrates. Subacute lesions consist of an atrophic epidermis with claw-like extension of epidermal rete around a well-circumscribed lichenoid infiltrate, mimicking lichen nitidus. Pustules are not a typical morphologic characteristic of pinpoint papular light eruption clinically or histologically, arguing against this diagnosis in this patient.Acute localized exanthematous pustulosis consists of acute-onset nonfollicular, sterile pustules on an erythematous background affecting the face, neck, and chest.7 Most cases have been linked to drug hypersensitivity, but some reports describe associations with viral infection or insect bites.7 These lesions are often accompanied by fever and neutrophilic leukocytosis. Histologically, acute localized exanthematous pustulosis presents as sterile subcorneal pustules with an edematous papillary dermis and mixed perivascular infiltrates. This patient s history was negative for recent illness, systemic medications, or fever, rendering this diagnosis unlikely.Several treatment strategies have been proposed for actinic folliculitis. Adapalene, a topical retinoid, has been successful in preventing flares with application beginning a few weeks before sun exposure.2 Other reports have described success with isotretinoin, including complete clearance and extended periods of remission.8 Newer reports have explored the role of narrowband UV-B phototherapy, but more research is needed to determine its utility.9
Question: A woman in her 20s was referred for evaluation of a 4-year history of recurrent pustular eruptions on her face, which initially presented while she was vacationing in Florida. She noted mild associated pruritus but denied burning sensation. The eruptions occurred intermittently, appearing as erythematous papules with prominent pustules, and were exacerbated by sunlight. On average, her lesions would resolve in 3 to 4 days with only mild background erythema by 5 to 7 days. Even mild sun exposure would induce the eruption, leading to frequent recurrences in summer months but long periods of remission in the fall through spring. Trials with various broad-spectrum sunscreens in combination with topical and oral antibiotics and topical ivermectin did not prevent flares or mitigate severity of eruptions when they occurred. She denied joint pain, fever, fatigue, changes in urine color, use of other topical or systemic medications, or presence of rashes elsewhere. She was otherwise healthy without other medical history.On physical examination, she was well appearing with numerous 1- to 2-mm monomorphous pustules over background erythema on the medial cheeks, chin, and nose (Figure, A). No nail fold capillary changes or mucosal lesions were observed. Serum analysis results for antinuclear, anti-SSA/Ro, and anti-SSB/La antibodies were negative. A 4-mm punch biopsy was taken from a representative lesion along the right jawline (Figure, B-D). What Is Your Diagnosis?

explanation: Biopsy showed necrotizing vasculitis of the small vessels in the upper dermis and endothelial proliferation of medium-sized vessel in the mid dermis (Figure, C). There was periappendageal and perivascular lymphohistiocytic infiltrate containing foamy macrophages (Figure, B). The infiltrate extended up to the subcutaneous fat. Fite-Faraco stain revealed acid-fast bacilli in endothelial cells and a bacterial index (BI) of 6+ (Figure, D).Slit skin smear revealed a BI of 6+. Hemogram showed a hemoglobin level of 10 g/dL (100 g/L), total leukocyte count of 8200/ L(8.2 109/L), and a platelet count of 239 000/ L. Results from liver and kidney function tests; serum electrolytes, routine blood glucose, and urine tests; and microscopic evaluation were within normal limits. Electrocardiography and chest radiography results were normal. Results from human immunodeficiency virus serology and venereal disease research laboratory tests were negative.The patient was started on multibacillary multidrug therapy (MB MDT) regimen (rifampicin, 600 mg once a month; dapsone, 100 mg/d, clofazimine, 300 mg once a month and 50 mg/d) along with intravenous antibiotics (ceftriaxone, 1 g twice daily). Oral prednisolone was started with a dose of 40 mg/d and tapered over 3 weeks. The ulcers were cleaned and dressed with silver sulfadiazine. The patient s condition improved over the next 3 weeks. He was discharged with the advice to continue MB MDT for 2 years under monthly follow-up.Lucio phenomenon is a type of reaction seen in diffuse form of lepromatous leprosy (LL) also called  pure and primitive diffuse lepromatosis. 1,2 The condition has been mainly described in Mexicans, with very rare reports from India.3-5Lucio leprosy is a nonnodular type of LL that presents as uniform, smooth, infiltrated skin, better felt than seen. The patients first notice numbness and impairment of sensation in the hands and feet; later, there is loss of eyebrows, eyelashes, and finally the body hair.2Lucio phenomenon appears as triangular or polygonal-shaped red patches, mainly on the extremities, that later become purpuric. The center of purpuric lesions becomes necrotic and ulcerated and finally develops a brown or black crust (eschar). On the legs, the ulceration may be larger with jagged edges surrounded by an inflammatory zone.1,2 There may be secondary pyoderma lesions and chronic cellulitis complicating the condition.2Clinically, Lucio phenomenon can resemble the necrotic variant of erythema nodosum leprosum (ENL). However, there are certain differentiating features. Lucio leprosy is a diffuse infiltration of the skin in which the reaction occurs, whereas ENL occurs in LL with plaques and nodules. Lucio phenomenon is seen in untreated patients, but ENL is more frequent in patients receiving antileprosy treatment. Ulcers in Lucio phenomenon are small and superficial, while ENL ulcers are deep and may result in fibrotic scars. ENL lesions may be accompanied by systemic complaints, such as fever, arthralgia, myalgia, neuritis, iridocyclitis, orchitis, lymphadenopathy, nephritis, and hepatitis; however, these signs and symptoms are minimal or totally absent in Lucio s phenomenon.6Lucio phenomenon can be differentiated histologically from ENL by features such as epidermal necrosis, necrotizing vasculitis of the superficial dermal vessels, severe focal endothelial proliferation of the mid-dermal vessels, and by the presence of large number of bacilli in endothelial cells.7Our patient had features of Lucio leprosy with numbness preceding the loss of eyebrows and skin infiltration. The symptoms of Lucio phenomenon developed 1  years after the onset of numbness. The clinical picture was characteristic, with necrotic lesions located chiefly on the extremities. Also, there was absence of fever, joint pain, and other constitutional symptoms. The histological findings of vasculitis of the small upper dermal vessels, endothelial proliferation of the mid-dermal vessel, and presence of acid-fast bacilli within the endothelial cells confirmed the diagnosis.MB-MDT remains the mainstay of treatment. Although steroids have a good effect, thalidomide is of no value.1 Our patient improved with MB-MDT, antibiotics, oral prednisolone, and local wound care.
Question: A man in his 30s had ulceration of the skin of the soles, legs, hands, elbow joints, and face of 15 days  duration. It had started as swelling of the feet, followed by erythematous acral lesions. Numbness of the hands and feet had begun 1  years previously, spreading gradually to the forearm and legs. He had no history of fever, joint pains, or epistaxis. Black eschar and ulceration with purulent discharge was seen over the soles and elbows. There were triangular and bizarre purpuric and necrotic lesions over the legs, thighs, and dorsum of the hands (Figure, A) with necrosis of pinnae and nasolabial folds. The scrotum was also affected. There was diffuse infiltration of the face and madarosis of the lateral half of the eyebrows. He also had hypoesthesia over the palms, proximal forearms, feet, and legs. Peripheral nerve examination revealed thickening of the right radial cutaneous and both ulnar nerves. Bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy was present. Findings from the rest of the general physical and systemic examination were normal. Punch biopsy specimens were obtained (Figure, B-D).A, Multiple bizarrely shaped purpuric and necrotic plaques on the hands and lower limbs. Erosion is seen on the scrotum. B-D, Histopathologic images. What Is Your Diagnosis?

explanation: Adenomatoid odontogenic tumor (AOT) is a noninvasive odontogenic tumor with approximately two-thirds associated with impacted canine teeth in the anterior maxilla and a female:male predilection of 1.9:1; as such, it is often referred to as the two-thirds tumor. Typically occurring in the second decade of life, an AOT originates from remnants of the dental lamina or enamel organ and is associated with mutations in the KRAS gene (mitogen-activated protein kinase pathway; p.G12V and p.G12R loci) in 70% of AOTs according to recent genetic data published in the fifth edition of the World Health Organization s classification of odontogenic and maxillofacial bone tumors.1,2 While 97.7% of all AOTs fall into either the follicular (71%) or extrafollicular (29%) subtypes of the central variant, a peripheral AOT (PAOT) is rare but possible, with 2.3% of all AOTs solely involving the gingival tissue overlying the alveolar ridge.3 The PAOT also demonstrates a much higher female predilection, with a reported female:male ratio of 6.3:1.4 The pathogenesis of a PAOT involves proliferation of epithelial remnants within the gingival tissue following tooth eruption in response to an unknown stimulus.Radiographically, follicular and extrafollicular AOTs appear as well-defined, circumscribed, and unilocular radiolucencies associated with unerupted teeth with varying degrees of calcification. As a PAOT occurs in soft tissue, there are no pathognomonic radiographic findings; however, erosion of the labial plate and displacement of teeth, as seen in this case, as well as deep periodontal pockets are not uncommon.5 Histologically, both central AOTs and PAOTs demonstrate ductlike structures arising from the cuboidal epithelial cell component of the lesion, with spindle-shaped epithelial cells interspersed with cementumlike calcifications or amyloidlike homogeneous eosinophilic material. Typically, AOTs are well encapsulated with connective tissue and, as such, are easily separated from adjacent anatomic structures; however, this encapsulation is usually not present in PAOTs.6-8 Due to their similar pathogenesis and cellular origin, many reactive proliferation lesions, including peripheral ossifying fibroma, peripheral giant cell granuloma, and pyogenic granuloma, as well as many peripheral odontogenic cysts or tumors, including peripheral ameloblastoma, odontogenic fibroma, gingival cyst, peripheral ameloblastic fibroma, peripheral odontoma, peripheral odontogenic keratocyst, and peripheral calcifying odontogenic cyst, can demonstrate similar gingival enlargement as seen with PAOTs.6-8 As a result of multiple lesions appearing similar clinically, definitive diagnosis is made only after histopathological examination.Treatment for a PAOT typically involves conservative surgical removal via enucleation and curettage of the lesion down to bone, resulting in very rare instances of recurrence of the tumor, with excellent prognosis.6,9 The present patient was followed up at 1 month at the Department of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery at the University of Illinois Chicago College of Dentistry, and subsequently at 6 months at the patient s initial referring dentist, with no evidence of lesion recurrence. While no appreciable spontaneous buccal movement of the malpositioned left maxillary lateral incisor was observed at 1-month follow-up, the patient was cleared to initiate orthodontic intervention.This case highlights the unique nature of odontogenic lesions to occur throughout the oral cavity and consideration of a broad differential diagnosis, because even benign-appearing lesions could potentially represent devastating diseases. Additionally, early diagnosis and intervention helped in limiting underlying bone loss and further displacement of tooth number 9, demonstrating that early detection and biopsy play important roles in therapeutic success.
Question: A 12-year-old boy with no relevant medical history presented with a left maxillary gingival swelling of 4 years  duration associated with palatal tipping of the left maxillary central incisor. The lesion reportedly grew over a 2-year period but remained unchanged over the past 2 years. The patient denied pain, bleeding, or neurosensory disturbances associated with the lesion or dentition. Intraoral examination was notable for a firm, 1 1-cm, pink, dome-shaped gingival mass extending onto the facial surface of tooth number 9, coalescing with a broader, pale-appearing, soft to firm gingival enlargement apical to the tooth (Figure, A). No edema, erythema, or signs of infection were appreciated, and the adjacent teeth demonstrated no mobility with normal periodontal probing depths and vitality. Extraoral examination was unremarkable. Radiographic examination showed no endodontic involvement or intralesional calcifications; however, cone-beam computed tomography imaging revealed saucerization of the facial bone near tooth number 9.A, Large, rounded submucosal lesion of the anterior maxilla facial and apical to tooth number 9. B, Low-power histomicrograph shows a nodule of fibrous connective tissue surfaced by keratinized stratified squamous epithelium (original magnification  5). The bulk of the specimen consists of densely to loosely arranged collagen bundles with scattered fibroblasts. Centrally, there is a focal proliferation of odontogenic epithelium (pink arrowhead). The inset shows a higher-power image with a proliferation of odontogenic epithelium composed of hyperchromatic ovoid cells that are arranged into pseudoductal structures (blue arrowheads; original magnification  20). Small basophilic calcifications also are identified within the epithelial proliferation (black arrowhead).Excisional biopsy of the lesion was performed, and microscopic examination of multiple sections revealed a nodule of keratinized superficial stratified squamous epithelium overlying fibrous connective tissue (Figure, B). The surface epithelium demonstrated a normal maturational pattern. The underlying fibrous connective tissue was composed of densely to loosely arranged collagen bundles interspersed with fibroblasts, blood vessels, and scattered chronic inflammatory cells. Notably, within the central portions of the fibrous nodule on deeper levels, there was a proliferation of odontogenic epithelium composed of mildly hyperchromatic ovoid cells arranged into pseudoducts (Figure, B). Small basophilic spherical calcifications were intermixed with the odontogenic epithelium. What Is Your Diagnosis?

caption: A, Shallow erosions with hemorrhagic crusts on the lips of a 22-year-old woman. B and C, Targetoid macules with dusky centers on the palms.
opb: Order a rapid plasma reagin test
opc: Perform a skin biopsy of a targetoid lesion on the palm for pathologic evaluation
opd: Perform a viral culture from a lip lesion
diagnosis: Herpes simplex virus (HSV) induced erythema multiforme
answer: Perform a viral culture from a lip lesion
explanation: D. Perform a viral culture from a lip lesionThe key clinical features in this case are oral ulcers and the classic targetoid lesions suggestive of erythema multiforme. Given that the patient was not taking any medications, an infectious etiology is likely, most commonly HSV or Mycoplasma pneumoniae. Further testing with viral culture, serologies, direct fluorescence antibody, or polymerase chain reaction (PCR) can be performed to determine the organism. Polymerase chain reaction is often the preferred method for diagnosis of HSV, and is recommended as the most sensitive and specific test by the American Society for Microbiology. However, PCR availability varies by institution. Because the patient presented on a weekend when this test was unavailable, viral culture was used for diagnosis.Erythema multiforme is an immune-mediated hypersensitivity reaction that presents with an acute skin eruption, which may be accompanied by oral lesions.1 The condition is classified into erythema multiforme major and erythema multiforme minor, which are distinguished by mucosal involvement or lack thereof, respectively. Up to 90% of cases are caused by infections, most commonly HSV and occasionally M pneumoniae. Medication-induced erythema multiforme, caused by agents such as nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, sulfonamides, antibiotics, and antiepileptics, accounts for less than 10% of cases. Less common associations include autoimmune disorders, malignancy, immunizations, radiation, and menstruation.1,2Erythema multiforme is clinically characterized by targetoid skin lesions that can be accompanied by mucocutaneous patches, erosions, or bullae.1,2 Target-like macules with a dusky center are usually pathognomonic for the disorder but may not always be present. The central area can also be bullous, and the Koebner phenomenon (development of lesions at sites of trauma or UV light) is occasionally observed. Lesions are most commonly found on the palms and soles in a symmetric fashion. The dorsal hands and feet can also be involved. In 10% of cases, widespread involvement of the trunk can occur. Erythema multiforme is usually asymptomatic, but some patients experience burning or pruritus.1,2The differential diagnosis includes reaction to arthropod bites and stings, Beh et disease, fixed drug eruption, erosive lichen planus, urticaria, acute febrile neutrophilic dermatosis (Sweet syndrome), polymorphous light eruption, and autoimmune blistering diseases (eg, bullous pemphigoid, paraneoplastic pemphigus). The diagnosis can usually be made with history and physical examination, but skin biopsy might be helpful for diagnosis if an infectious or iatrogenic etiology cannot be identified. Chest radiography may help to diagnose underlying M pneumoniae. Herpes simplex virus infection should be ruled out initially in patients presenting with erythema multiforme. A rapid plasma reagin test for syphilis is indicated for any patient with a palmar or plantar rash. However, the appearance of targetoid dusky macules on the palms is clinically more consistent with erythema multiforme.Herpes simplex virus titers (IgG, IgM) are a rapid way to make the diagnosis, if positive; however, it is difficult to distinguish recurrences using serologic testing alone. Viral culture has higher sensitivity than serology for HSV diagnosis when an adequate sample is collected, but results may take longer. Material for culture should be obtained from oral ulcerations during viral shedding, and cultures are more likely to be positive if taken early during the course of disease. Ideally, material should be obtained from the base of a newly deroofed vesicle. Polymerase chain reaction is the gold standard for diagnosis of HSV infection but may not be available at all institutions. Skin biopsy is typically unnecessary because erythema multiforme is a clinical diagnosis. Additionally, a skin biopsy would likely show only erythema multiforme, without differentiating the underlying cause of this condition.Infection-induced erythema multiforme is typically self-limited and will resolve in about 2 to 3 weeks without sequelae. Recurrences can occur and are usually caused by HSV.1,2 Treatment is primarily supportive care and involves addressing the underlying illness or discontinuing the causative medication.1,2 Antiviral agents can be used for treatment of underlying HSV infection, with recommendations to use sunscreen-containing lip balm and lotion to prevent UV light induced outbreaks of HSV.3,4 For patients with recurrent HSV outbreaks, long-term suppressive doses of antivirals may be necessary and can prevent recurrence of erythema multiforme in the majority of cases. Mycoplasma pneumoniae infection can be treated with azithromycin, which will help to improve the associated erythema multiforme. Recalcitrant cases are associated with an inability to identify the underlying cause of erythema multiforme.3,4A viral culture obtained from a lip lesion was positive for HSV. Further laboratory investigation showed normal M pneumoniae serologic results and elevated levels of HSV IgG and IgM. The patient was started on oral valacyclovir, and the skin lesions resolved without further episodes.
Question: A 22-year-old woman presents with oral ulcers and a rash on her palms of 4 days  duration. She does not take any prescribed or over-the-counter medications. Review of systems is noncontributory, and she denies fevers, chills, shortness of breath, cough, and genital lesions. Vital signs are normal, and physical examination reveals shallow erosions with hemorrhagic crusts on the lips and widespread targetoid macules with dusky centers on the palms (Figure). The remainder of the examination is unremarkable.Perform a skin biopsy of a targetoid lesion on the palm for pathologic evaluationPerform a viral culture from a lip lesion What Would You Do Next?

caption: Elongated filiform papillae and black discoloration of patient s tongue.
opc: Provide reassurance and prescribe oral care
answer: Provide reassurance and prescribe oral care
explanation: The key to the correct diagnosis is recognizing that black tongue discoloration in an older male patient undergoing treatment with antimicrobial therapy is characteristic of black hairy tongue. Choice A is incorrect because the patient did not have physical signs or symptoms consistent with Addison disease. A tongue biopsy (choice B) is an invasive procedure not typically needed to make the diagnosis of black hairy tongue. Discontinuing cefazolin (choice D) is not recommended because this antibiotic is the preferred treatment for methicillin-susceptible S aureus bacteremia and septic arthritis of the shoulder.Black hairy tongue, also known as lingua villosa nigra, is characterized by discoloration of the dorsal surface of the tongue and hypertrophied, filiform papillae that are longer than 3 mm and wider than 2 mm and that appear like hair growth on the tongue.1,2 Although patients typically have a black-colored tongue, the tongue may appear brown or, less commonly, yellow, green, or blue.3 Most patients with black hairy tongue are asymptomatic, but some may present with halitosis, dysgeusia, gagging, nausea, xerostomia, and pain or burning of the tongue.1,4 The diagnosis of black hairy tongue is typically made by visual inspection, but dermoscopy is sometimes used to evaluate the filiform papillae.1-3The exact pathophysiology of black hairy tongue remains unclear, but the condition may be caused by defective desquamation of the dorsal tongue, leading to accumulation of keratinized layers that elongate the filiform papillae.1 These papillae accumulate bacteria, fungi, and residue from food, coffee, tea, and tobacco.1,4 Discoloration of the tongue may also be caused by porphyrin-producing chromogenic bacteria or yeast, although no specific bacteria or yeast have been identified as causing black hairy tongue.1,2Factors associated with black hairy tongue include certain behaviors, such as smoking or chewing tobacco, alcohol use, heavy black tea consumption, illicit drug use, poor dental hygiene, and excessive use of oxidizing mouth washes that contain sodium perborate, sodium peroxide, and hydrogen peroxide.1 Medications associated with black hairy tongue include many antibiotics, antipsychotic drugs that cause xerostomia (eg, olanzapine and chlorpromazine), and some chemotherapeutic agents (eg, erlotinib).1-3,5 Patients who are edentulous or who have HIV, advanced cancer, graft-vs-host disease, trigeminal neuralgia, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, or chronic xerostomia, or who have received recent radiation therapy to the head or neck, are at increased risk of black hairy tongue.1,2,4 Black hairy tongue has also been reported in patients with COVID-19.6Although the exact prevalence of black hairy tongue is unknown and varies among populations, studies of dental and oral medicine outpatients have reported a prevalence of 1.2% to 11.3%.4,7-9 Black hairy tongue is 3 times more common in males than females, which may be due to higher rates of cigarette smoking and poor oral hygiene among men.1 The prevalence of black hairy tongue also increases with advancing age, especially after age 60 years.1Black hairy tongue should be differentiated from pseudo black hairy tongue, a condition in which the tongue is darkly stained due to medications (eg, bismuth subsalicylate or minocycline), chemicals, or food coloring, but the filiform papillae are not enlongated.2,4 The differential diagnosis of black hairy tongue includes pigmented fungiform papillae of the tongue, acanthosis nigricans, congenital lingual melanotic macules, oral hairy leukoplakia, congenital melanocytic nevi, premalignant leukoplakia, squamous cell carcinoma, and hypertrophic herpes simplex virus infection.2Aside from cosmetic concerns, black hairy tongue is benign. Treatment consists of providing reassurance to the patient, removing possible causative factors, gentle debridement with a toothbrush or tongue scraper, and improved oral hygiene.4 Application of topical baking soda or diluted hydrogen peroxide helps with desquamation of the hyperkeratotic papillae.1 Black hairy tongue generally resolves within days to weeks after mechanical debridement and removal of precipitating factors. Patients who do not respond to these treatments may undergo papillae clipping or removal by carbon dioxide laser or electrodessication.1The patient was instructed to brush his tongue twice daily with a soft toothbrush. Within 2 weeks, black hairy tongue resolved without interruption of intravenous cefazolin therapy. He had no recurrence of black hairy tongue within 6 months after initial hospitalization and has since been lost to follow-up.
Question: An 80-year-old man presented to the emergency department with a 5-day history of fever and right shoulder pain. His temperature was 37.9  C (100.2  F); heart rate, 74/min; and blood pressure, 90/49 mm Hg. He had tenderness to palpation and decreased range of motion of his right shoulder. Laboratory testing showed a white blood cell count of 9.6 109/L (reference, 4.0-9.0 109/L); C-reactive protein level, 35.6 mg/dL (reference, <0.3 mg/dL), and creatine kinase level, 1036 U/L (17.30  kat/L) (reference, 60-270 U/L [1.00-4.51  kat/L]). Magnetic resonance imaging revealed an effusion and synovial thickening with enhancement in the right shoulder joint and inflammation in the right subscapularis muscle. Gram stain of the synovial fluid revealed gram-positive cocci in clusters. Intravenous cefazolin therapy was initiated, and he underwent right shoulder joint arthroscopic irrigation, synovectomy, and surgical drainage for subscapularis pyomyositis. Blood and synovial fluid cultures grew methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus, and intravenous cefazolin was continued. One week after hospitalization, the patient developed asymptomatic black discoloration of the dorsal surface of his tongue (Figure). He reported no prior lingual discoloration and did not smoke cigarettes, use chewing tobacco or illicit drugs, or drink alcohol. What Would You Do Next?

caption: Preoperative photograph shows orthotropic eye position during a period of inattention. Note the right esotropia and right dissociated vertical divergence during left eye fixation at near (left inset) and larger left esotropia during right eye fixation at near (right inset).
Clinical photograph of the patient s eyes while she was under nondepolarizing paralyzing anesthesia shows the orthotropic position of the eyes under conditions of noninnervation.
explanation: The term dissociated horizontal deviation (DHD) was originally used to describe a unique form of intermittent exotropia in which the measured exodeviation was larger with 1 eye fixating than the other.1-4 In some cases, the exodeviation resolves or converts to a large esodeviation when the patient fixates with the nonpreferred eye. Associated clinical findings include latent nystagmus, dissociated vertical divergence, and sensorial suppression of the nonfixating eye, indicating that DHD develops only when binocular visual input is preempted early in life.3-6 Some cases arise spontaneously, but most patients have undergone previous bimedial recessions.5Originally attributed to unequal convergence damping of latent nystagmus,1,2 it is now clear that this variable exodeviation is driven by a change in the sensory balance of visual inputs from both eyes.7 In patients with DHD, monocular fixation induces a dissociated esodeviation (greater during fixation with the nonpreferred eye) that is usually superimposed on an exotropic resting position of the eyes (as found under nondepolarizing general anesthesia). Thus, when the baseline vergence position of the eyes is orthotropic, DHD can generate an esodeviation of variable amplitude in both eyes. Gr f et al8 described this phenomenon in a patient who presented with unilateral accommodative esotropia. This patient s eyes were aligned when examined under nondepolarizing anesthesia (Figure 2), confirming that DHD must arise from excessive esotonus during conditions of monocular fixation. The oscillopsia resulted from her latent nystagmus, which manifested during periods of intense fixation and esodeviation.Clinical photograph of the patient s eyes while she was under nondepolarizing paralyzing anesthesia shows the orthotropic position of the eyes under conditions of noninnervation.The initial confirmatory test for DHD is the reversed fixation test (choice C).9 After measuring the horizontal deviation in the nonfixating eye using alternate cover testing, the occluder is shifted back to the nonfixating eye, which normally shows no movement because the horizontal deviation has already been neutralized by the prism. In DHD, however, a horizontal movement of the fixating eye is seen, showing the presence of fixation-dependent innervation.9,10The differential diagnosis of intermittent esotropia in adulthood includes decompensating esotropia (often caused by early presbyopia, necessitating excessive accommodative effort), anisometropia, and DHD. Retinoscopy ruled out anisometropia. An esodeviation produced by excessive accommodative effort owing to early presbyopia would be symmetrical in both eyes. Forced duction testing (choice A) was not indicated as ocular rotations were full. The absence of diplopia and the presence of latent nystagmus showed that this esotropia arose from a long-standing strabismus rather than a new-onset neurologic condition. Magnetic resonance imaging (choice B) was therefore not warranted. An increase in the exodeviation during periods of inattention (ie, nonfixation) is a useful clinical clue to the diagnosis of DHD.10 In this patient, resolution of the esodeviation (due to suspension of dissociated esotonus) was seen during periods of nonfixation. Genetic testing (choice D) was not indicated as infantile strabismus is rarely hereditary, and she had no family history of strabismus.The patient was treated with small bilateral lateral rectus resections, which produced a consecutive exotropia. After small bilateral lateral rectus recessions, her eyes were straight at distance and near, and her symptoms abated.
Question: A 33-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of intermittent esotropia. When playing video games at a distance or when reading, she developed oscillopsia with eyestrain and noted that her right eye turned up and in. She had undergone strabismus surgery for crossed eyes as a young child and more recently had laser in situ keratomileusis performed. She was medically healthy and neurodevelopmentally normal but had been born 4  weeks premature. There was no family history of strabismus, and she denied diplopia.On examination, corrected visual acuity was 20/30 2 OD and 20/15 OS. Both pupils reacted briskly to light with no afferent pupillary defect. She had 1+ apparent overaction of the right inferior oblique and superior rectus muscles. When she was inattentive, her eyes appeared to be straight (Figure 1). Prism and alternate cover testing during near fixation showed a right esotropia and dissociated vertical divergence when fixing with her preferred left eye (Figure 1, left inset) and a larger left esotropia during forced fixation with her right eye (Figure 1, right inset). She had no cycloplegic refractive error, and retinal examination showed no objective torsion. Placement of +3.00 lenses eliminated her esotropia at near but could not be used to alleviate her symptoms at distance, showing a high accommodation-to-convergence ratio. What Would You Do Next?

opa: The patient has normal BP since the office BP was normal.
opb: The patient has masked uncontrolled hypertension.
opc: The patient has white coat hypertension.
opd: The patient s BP dips appropriately during sleep.
answer: The patient has masked uncontrolled hypertension.
explanation: Twenty-four-hour ambulatory BP monitoring measures BP over 24 hours, every 15 to 30 minutes during the day, and every 30 to 60 minutes during sleep (eTable in the Supplement). Observational studies consistently demonstrated that ambulatory BP monitoring is more predictive of clinical outcomes than  office BP measurement.1-4 Testing is indicated when clinicians suspect that office BP does not reflect ambulatory BP levels. White coat hypertension may be suspected in patients with office BP levels of 140/90 mm Hg to 169/99 mm Hg, who are nonsmokers, and who report anxiety at clinic visits; however, if patients with these characteristics are smokers, it is less likely that a clinician would suspect white coat hypertension because sustained hypertension is more common. Patients with normal office BP but hypertension-related end-organ damage, such as left ventricular hypertrophy, may have elevated ambulatory BP levels. A normal office BP without antihypertensive medication in a patient with elevated ambulatory BP is defined as having masked hypertension. Patients who meet criteria for masked hypertension and who are taking antihypertensive medication are classified as having masked uncontrolled hypertension.The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services covers ambulatory BP monitoring to evaluate white coat hypertension. The prevalence of white coat hypertension is 10% to 35% among patients evaluated for hypertension.2,4,5 Medicare reimbursement is $50 to $70.6 Some private insurers cover ambulatory BP monitors to evaluate additional phenotypes (see eTable in the Supplement), such as masked hypertension.1,2 Over a 10-year period, more than 40% of adults with white coat or masked hypertension may develop sustained hypertension.7The patient s hypertension was associated with episodes of anxiety and high-sodium diet. Common causes of ambulatory hypertension include sleep disorders, medication nonadherence, tobacco use, and psychosocial stressors.8 Adults with masked hypertension or masked uncontrolled hypertension are more than twice as likely to develop cardiovascular disease as normotensive adults.4,9 Treatment should be based on potential etiologies (for this patient, anxiety and a high-sodium diet) and does not always require antihypertensive drugs.Normally, nocturnal BP decreases between 10% and 19%. Decreases of 10% or less are considered as inadequate (known as nondipping). Elevated morning BP levels, as read on home BP monitors, can suggest inadequate dipping. In a registry of 17 312 adults with hypertension, nondipping was associated with a 27% higher risk of cardiovascular events.10 Proposed explanations include increased sympathetic tone, arterial stiffness, or salt sensitivity. Strategies to treat nondipping include administering medication at night and treating sleep disorders. However, no randomized trials have evaluated whether restoring dipping with therapeutic interventions improves outcomes.Ambulatory BP monitoring could be repeated to confirm BP control in high-risk patients. Home BP monitoring, if performed correctly, provides similar information as daytime ambulatory BP monitors.2 Patients with white coat hypertension may benefit from repeat testing every 1 to 2 years to screen for sustained hypertension.1Home BP monitors are a reasonable alternative for patients without access to ambulatory BP monitoring. Results of home BP monitors are independently associated with cardiovascular risk, after adjusting for office BP.2,3 Advantages of ambulatory BP monitoring are that it includes nocturnal measurements, facilitates measurement outside of the home, does not rely on patient technique, and may be better tolerated by patients who experience anxiety with BP measurement. In contrast, home BP monitoring allows for BP measurement over more than 1 day and can also help improve BP control.2The patient was encouraged to follow a low-sodium diet. She began a regimen of amlodipine (5 mg) in the evening to restore nocturnal dipping. Because some of her hypertensive episodes were associated with anxiety or mental stress, paroxetine was increased to 40 mg, and she was referred for counseling. Her daytime BP control improved, based on home BP monitoring. Her anxiety remained difficult to manage. The large increases in BP level during panic attacks continued, although her systolic BP rarely reached 160 mm Hg. A repeat ambulatory BP monitoring would have been reasonable, but was not performed.Ambulatory BP monitoring is most often used to evaluate white coat hypertension.Progression from white coat hypertension to sustained hypertension is common.Additional hypertension phenotypes detected with ambulatory BP monitors include masked hypertension and nocturnal nondipping.Causes of masked hypertension or nondipping should be evaluated and treated accordingly (eg, high-sodium diet, sleep disorders, and psychosocial stress).
Question: A 65-year-old woman presented to her primary care physician with concerns about large fluctuations in her blood pressure (BP). The patient s home BP measurements were often lower than 120/80 mm Hg but sometimes reached 200/100 mm Hg. She had a non ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction 1 year ago and was treated with a drug-eluting stent in her right coronary artery. Her antihypertensive medications included carvedilol (12.5 mg, twice daily), lisinopril (40 mg daily), and chlorthalidone (12.5 mg daily). Additionally, the patient had generalized anxiety and panic disorders (treated with paroxetine, 30 mg), and she smoked for 90 pack-years but quit 2 months ago. The patient s office BP measurement was 127/74 mm Hg. Her physical examination was unremarkable.Twenty-four-hour ambulatory BP monitoring was performed to evaluate patterns of elevated BP and potential etiologies. The patient kept a log of her diet, sleep pattern, and symptoms. Mean BP levels were 133/86 mm Hg overall (normal, <130/80 mm Hg); daytime, 135/88 mm Hg (normal, <135/85 mm Hg); and sleeping, 124/78 mm Hg (normal, <120/70 mm Hg).1 During the first sudden increase in BP (04:00-06:00; Figure), she awoke from sleep with a panic attack. The second sudden increase (07:00-10:00) occurred after eating a high-sodium breakfast.The patient has normal BP since the office BP was normal. How Do You Interpret These Results?

caption: Lead II electrocardiogram rhythm strip.
Illustration of atrial conduction and corresponding electrocardiographic (ECG) features during atrial fibrillation.
opa: Review the ECG for frequent atrial ectopic beats
opb: Review the ECG for features of atrial fibrillation
opc: Review the ECG for atrial flutter with varying AV block
opd: Review the ECG for features of multifocal atrial tachycardia
answer: Review the ECG for features of atrial fibrillation
explanation: The key to the correct diagnosis in this case is the absence of P waves, irregularly irregular R-R intervals, and fibrillary (f) waves on the ECG tracing. Careful analysis of the tracing is required to establish these features of atrial fibrillation (AF). Other dysrhythmias that have a similar appearance include frequent atrial ectopic beats, wandering atrial pacemaker, multifocal atrial tachycardia, and atrial flutter with varying AV block. These conditions may be distinguished from AF on the ECG by the fact that organized atrial activity would be present and denoted by the presence of distinct P waves, which are not seen on this tracing.Atrial fibrillation is the most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia and has a wide range of complications including stroke, heart failure, myocardial infarction, sudden cardiac death, chronic kidney disease, cognitive dysfunction, and mortality.1 A single standard 12-lead ECG is often enough to confirm the diagnosis if the patient has permanent AF. However, long-term rhythm monitoring (eg, Holter monitoring, event monitoring, telemetry patches, implantable loop recorders) may be needed in patients with or suspected to have intermittent paroxysmal AF.2 Because the automated detection of AF by current ECG interpretation software is not always accurate, physician overreading is required. Incorrect interpretation of the ECG in AF may result in inappropriate medical treatment and misuse of resources.3The ECG features of AF often allow for accurate diagnosis, but there are several pitfalls.4 These pitfalls may be avoided by understanding how these ECG features of AF are related to the pathophysiology of the condition. At the cellular level, AF is characterized by chaotic atrial electrical activity in the form of several scattered waves of fast and irregular impulses generated by microreentrant circuits within the atria and around the pulmonary veins.5 This lack of uniform, consistent activation of atrial tissue initiating from the sinus node is the crucial factor in creating the 3 primary diagnostic features of AF on the ECG: absence of P waves, irregularly irregular R-R intervals, and fibrillary (f) waves.6Figure 2 is an illustration of atrial conduction during AF and the corresponding ECG features.Illustration of atrial conduction and corresponding electrocardiographic (ECG) features during atrial fibrillation.The P wave on the ECG represents atrial tissue activation. In AF, the disorganized atrial activation creates high-frequency, low-amplitude electrical impulses that partially cancel one another. This leads to an absence of distinctive P waves on the ECG as well as ineffective mechanical function of the atrial tissue. As a consequence of rapid atrial activation during AF, the sinus node becomes completely suppressed and plays no part in activating the atrium.P waves disappear once AF occurs; in contrast, fibrillatory f waves appear. These f waves are the ECG manifestation of the fast, weak atrial tissue activation; hence, they are of various amplitudes and occur at rapid rates (300-600/min) with irregular intervals. Fibrillatory f waves are typically more prominent in lead V1 and the inferior leads (II, III, and aVF) and tend to have greater amplitudes in recent-onset AF (coarse AF). In some cases, this phenomenon can make AF look like an atrial flutter. Given that the morphology of the f waves may be mimicked by extracardiac artifacts (eg, electrical noise, tremors), such artifacts may give a false impression of the presence of f waves. In contrast, in patients with marked atrial fibrosis and AF of long duration, f waves may manifest as an undulating ECG baseline or may be of such low amplitudes that they are unrecognizable. In such cases, the ECG diagnosis of AF may be inferred by the absence of any distinctive P waves and the presence of an irregularly irregular R-R interval.The irregularly irregular rhythm in AF observed as an irregularly irregular R-R interval on the ECG is a net product of the erratic, rapid atrial activity and the conduction properties of the atrioventricular (AV) node. Typically, after each atrial stimulus, the AV node does not allow conduction for a period of time, termed the effective refractory period of the AV node. By preventing the conduction of atrial impulses arriving at the AV node during the effective refractory period, the AV node acts as a brake that protects the ventricles from surges of fast supraventricular arrhythmias. In the case of AF, the high frequency of atrial impulses arriving at the AV node at irregular intervals leads to variable impulse conduction through the AV node. This is reflected in the ECG by irregularly irregular R-R intervals, with a ventricular response rate that typically ranges from 90/min to 170/min.7 The QRS complexes are usually narrow if the His-Purkinje system is intact and the patient does not have conduction abnormalities or ventricular preexcitation.7These ECG features, however, may be distorted in certain clinical scenarios. The R-R interval may be regular in AF in patients with complete AV block with either junctional (narrow QRS) or ventricular (wide QRS) escape rhythm, resulting in a slow, regular ventricular response. Also, in patients with AF and rapid ventricular response rate the R-R intervals may become very short, giving the gross visual impression of a regular rhythm. The use of calipers to carefully measure all of the R-R intervals on the ECG tracing may help differentiate AF from a paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia such as atrial tachycardia, AV nodal reentrant tachycardia, or orthodromic reentrant tachycardia.Taken together, the ECG features of AF (absence of P waves, irregularly irregular R-R intervals, and f waves) enable the correct diagnosis of AF in most cases. However, there are scenarios in which these criteria do not perfectly discriminate AF from other atrial rhythms. Physicians should be mindful of these potential pitfalls to avoid making errors in the diagnosis of AF, which could have serious consequences for their patients.
Question: A 65-year-old man who had been treated for hypertension for the past 10 years presented with increasing fatigue, dizziness, and palpitations. He did not have chest pain or any other symptoms. An electrocardiogram (ECG) was obtained (Figure 1).Review the ECG for frequent atrial ectopic beatsReview the ECG for features of atrial fibrillationReview the ECG for atrial flutter with varying AV blockReview the ECG for features of multifocal atrial tachycardia What Would You Do Next?

caption: Slitlamp photograph of the patient s right eye with posterior synechiae, hypopyon, and rubeosis iridis (arrowheads) after mydriasis.
A hypopyon cytology test (Giemsa; original magnification  400) revealed mostly numerous small, atypical lymphocytes with a high nucleus-to-cytoplasm ratio and irregular nuclei (arrowheads).
opa: Anterior chamber tap for a multiplex polymerase chain reaction test for infectious uveitis
opb: Anterior chamber tap for hypopyon cytology tests
answer: Anterior chamber tap for hypopyon cytology tests
explanation: Hypopyon cytology testing revealed numerous small, atypical lymphocytes (Figure 2). The patient was later diagnosed with Alzheimer disease. It was discovered that the patient had self-discontinued all the medications that he should have been taking (including TKI and antidiabetic drugs) 8 months prior, owing to decreased cognitive function. Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain and a lumbar puncture revealed no further central nervous system involvement. Messenger RNA from the breakpoint cluster region tyrosine-protein kinase ABL1 (BCR-ABL) gene was not detected in the peripheral blood. The patient was diagnosed with an intraocular relapse of Philadelphia chromosome positive ALL.A hypopyon cytology test (Giemsa; original magnification  400) revealed mostly numerous small, atypical lymphocytes with a high nucleus-to-cytoplasm ratio and irregular nuclei (arrowheads).Philadelphia chromosome positive ALL is the most frequent chromosomal abnormality in adult ALL (20%-30% of adults with ALL).1 Ocular manifestations of leukemia (cotton-wool spots, Roth spots, optic nerve involvement, and serous retinal detachments) can be caused by direct intraocular infiltration or associated comorbidities.2 Anterior chamber infiltration constitutes approximately 12% in masquerade syndrome and is seen in up to 18% of relapsed cases of ALL but rarely in adults.3,4 Few cases of rubeosis iridis in ocular leukemia have been reported.5 Pseudohypopyon, which is typically viscous and may be blood stained, occurs as a consequence of sequestration of leukemic cells along the long posterior ciliary vessels and the vessels of the iris into the anterior chamber.2,6 An acute rise of intraocular pressure can occur as a result of infiltration of the iris and trabecular meshwork.7Owing to the absence of ocular trauma or surgery and systemic or ocular findings, an anterior chamber tap for multiplex polymerase chain reaction for infectious uveitis (choice A) was not the optimal answer. Subconjunctival injection of dexamethasone (choice D) before ruling out an infection, such as tuberculosis, would be a premature decision. With the lack of associated systemic symptoms and mobile hypopyon, human leukocyte antigen typing (choice C), suspecting Beh et disease or syndromes associated with human leukocyte antigen B27, was not recommended as the next step. Ocular involvement in leukemia can be asymptomatic, leading to delayed treatment and poor prognosis systemically. In cases of apparent manifestations, such as pseudohypopyon, anterior chamber taps for cytology should be considered.Although development of TKIs has improved the prognosis of patients with Philadelphia chromosome positive ALL, the 5-year overall survival rate remains less than 20% for older patients.8 Conventional chemotherapy or external beam radiotherapy is usually the treatment of choice in an ocular relapse of ALL; however, their adverse effects can affect the quality of life and the prognosis remains poor.2,4,8 Intravitreal methotrexate (400 mg/0.1 mg) injections for the treatment of ocular leukemia, using the treatment strategy for primary vitreoretinal lymphoma (twice weekly in the first month, once weekly in the following 2 months, and once monthly thereafter), have been reported.9,10Considering the risk of adverse effects of systemic therapy, the patient was treated solely with dasatinib (a TKI), 70 mg, twice daily, and intravitreal methotrexate injections, which were administered less frequently, according to patient s preference (twice weekly for 2 weeks, monthly for 3 months, and then once every 2 months). The pseudohypopyon and rubeosis iridis resolved completely after 2 injections. His visual acuity improved to 20/40 OD (with a cataract), and his intraocular pressure normalized. He achieved ocular remission, and his regular hematological work-up results have remained negative for systemic relapse.
Question: A 78-year-old man presented with decreased visual acuity and hypopyon in his right eye. He had a history of Philadelphia chromosome positive acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), Stevens-Johnson syndrome (attributable to treatment with the tyrosine-kinase inhibitor [TKI] imatinib), and diabetes. He had senile dementia but did not complain of constitutional symptoms. His visual acuity was 20/80 OD and 20/32 OS, and his intraocular pressures were 31 mm Hg OD and 16 mm Hg OS. A slitlamp examination of the right eye showed redness, with small, white, nongranulomatous keratic precipitate as well as a cataract. His iris was diffusely thickened and the anterior chamber was slightly shallow, with 4+ cells and white hypopyon, which did not change position with head movements (Figure 1). There was also an extensive rubeosis iridis (Figure 1) with posterior synechiae in the right eye. The left anterior chamber was normal. A fundus examination had unremarkable results in both eyes. A complete blood cell count had normal results, and his hemoglobin A1c level was 16.5%. A syphilis test result was negative.Anterior chamber tap for a multiplex polymerase chain reaction test for infectious uveitis What Would You Do Next?

caption: Hematoma of the left true vocal fold and laryngeal surface of the epiglottis. B, CT indicates computed tomography.
explanation: Given the presence of pain and hematoma noted on laryngoscopy, a computed tomographic (CT) study of the neck was obtained, which showed a left nondisplaced vertical paramedian fracture of the thyroid cartilage (Figure B). The patient was treated conservatively with a methylprednisolone taper (starting dose of 20 mg) and voice rest. At his follow-up 2 weeks later, there was complete return of vocal quality and resolution of neck pain and odynophagia. Fiber-optic exam demonstrated near-complete resolution of the left true vocal fold edema and ecchymosis.The most frequent cause of thyroid cartilage fracture is blunt trauma to the neck, as seen in motor vehicle crashes, sports injuries, and strangulation. Nontraumatic thyroid cartilage fracture is exceedingly rare, and to our knowledge has been reported in the medical literature on only 4 occasions.1-4 All reported cases have involved male patients in their third to fifth decade. In all cases, including the present, the fracture occurred following a sudden increase in subglottic pressure from sneeze or cough. Odynophagia and dysphonia were present in all described cases.Symptoms and signs may include dysphagia, and swelling or crepitus overlying the thyroid cartilage. On flexible laryngoscopy, all patients displayed some degree of supraglottic and/or glottic edema with normal vocal fold movement. Of the 4 cases with imaging, all showed either a nondisplaced or minimally displaced paramedian fracture of the thyroid. Every patient had full resolution of their symptoms and physical examination findings with conservative treatment consisting of voice rest, steroids, and, in some cases, antibiotics.Previous reports have proposed hypotheses to explain this rare entity. Sneezing and coughing result in a sudden increase in subglottic pressure against a closed glottis. This mechanism of injury is fundamentally different from external trauma because fracture occurs from barotrauma exerting force outward as opposed to external trauma exerting force inward. It has been proposed that the patients who have sustained thyroid cartilage may have an underlying defect predisposing them to injury.3 Fenig and colleagues4 surmise that the mineralization and ossification process may have been abnormal in these patients. They4 further state that this process differs between males and females and, therefore, may explain the male predominance seen in these case reports. Thyroid cartilage in males ossifies earlier and more extensively than thyroid cartilage in females. Perhaps the patients in these reports have more extensively ossified thyroid cartilage compared with their peers. Alternatively, regions of highly ossified cartilage abutting nonossified cartilage could create areas susceptible to fracture with sudden increase in upper airway pressure.Nontraumatic fracture of the thyroid cartilage is an extremely rare entity. Patients giving a history of sneeze or cough followed by the acute onset of dysphonia, odynophagia, dysphagia, or neck pain warrant careful examination, including laryngoscopy and imaging. In most cases, assuming the fracture is nondisplaced, these patients can be treated conservatively with voice rest with full resolution of their symptoms.
Question: A man in his 30s presented to the emergency department with sudden voice change, odynophagia, and left-sided neck pain following a single sneeze. He experienced a popping sensation during the sneeze followed by neck stiffness 15 minutes later. He did not have associated shortness of breath, hemoptysis, or other pertinent medical history. He had no history of trauma, surgery, or intubation. On physical examination, his vital signs were stable with normal oxygen saturation on room air. His voice was rough and without stridor. Anterior neck examination revealed tenderness over the thyroid notch and left side of the laryngotracheal complex. There was no palpable crepitus. Flexible laryngoscopy was performed, which showed hematoma of the left true vocal fold and laryngeal surface of the epiglottis with normal vocal fold movement and complete glottic closure (Figure, A). What Is Your Diagnosis?

caption: A, Fundus photograph of the left eye with normal retinal vessels and linear lines in the posterior pole (arrowhead). B, Fluorescein angiography of the left eye with alternating light and dark patterns in the posterior pole.
Vertical axial B-scan ultrasonography of the left eye without masses, thickening of the sclera, or fluid in the Tenon space. There is straightening of the globe posteriorly.
explanation: The patient presented with metamorphopsia and was found to have choroidal folds on ophthalmoscopy in the left eye (Figure 1). The A-scan showed decreased axial length in the left eye compared with the right eye (right eye, 24.93 mm; left eye, 24.33 mm), which is consistent with the choroidal folds in the left eye. On B-scan, there were no masses, thickening of the sclera, or fluid in the Tenon space; however, there was straightening of both globes (left eye>right eye) posteriorly (Figure 2).Vertical axial B-scan ultrasonography of the left eye without masses, thickening of the sclera, or fluid in the Tenon space. There is straightening of the globe posteriorly.There is no published literature citing an association between bupropion and choroidal folds to date. He had 2 previous orbital MRIs on 2 separate occasions, which did not show any intraocular/orbital tumors or extraocular muscle thickness. On reviewing the most recent MRI, flattening of the globes was noted posteriorly (left>right).Choroidal folds are parallel undulations in the Bruch membrane, RPE, and inner choroid that lead to the appearance of lines, grooves, or striae.1 They create alternate yellow and dark streaks in the posterior pole, which are most commonly horizontal.1,2 There are many etiologies of choroidal folds including extraocular conditions (retrobulbar mass lesion, thyroid eye disease, and pseudotumor), inflammatory conditions (scleritis, uveitis, and Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada disease), choroidal diseases (choroidal tumors, effusion, scars, and neovascularization), and other entities including hypotony, papilledema, extraocular hardware (scleral buckle), trauma, and idiopathic (no known cause).1,2Choroidal folds represent a clinical sign that should prompt further investigation for systemic or associated diseases. Optical coherence tomography with EDI may help to differentiate between choroidal, chorioretinal, and retinal folds, as well as determines whether there is subretinal fluid or choroidal vascular lesions.2 Fluorescein angiography will show a hyperfluorescent crest because of increased choroidal fluorescence from stretched RPE cells and hypofluorescent troughs due to blockage from compressed RPE cells.2Ultrasonography not only determines axial length, but also can detect flattening of the globe posteriorly (Figure 2), intraocular masses and thickening of posterior sclera/choroid, or fluid in the Tenon space, which can occur in posterior scleritis. The intraocular pressures were normal in each eye, ruling out hypotony. Fluorescein angiography did not reveal any vascular choroidal tumors but did show alternating light and dark patterns in the left eye. These folds were seen on OCT with EDI. There was no history of extraocular hardware. Imaging is indicated to look for orbital processes such as thyroid eye disease and orbital masses.Intracranial hypertension can rarely cause acquired choroidal folds.3 This patient denied headaches, and visual field testing did not show any enlargement of the blind spot and fluorescein angiography did not show any leakage at the disk.Leahey et al4 performed a retrospective study on 78 eyes comparing unilateral (56%) and bilateral (44%) cases. The most common causes were scleritis (5 of 30 eyes) and age-related macular degeneration (14 of 48) for unilateral and bilateral cases, respectively. A case series on 57 eyes found the most common associations were hypertension, hyperopia, and autoimmune disease.5 Cangemi et al6 looked at 59 eyes and found the most common association to be hyperopia.Idiopathic choroidal folds are usually found in men with hyperopia with normal visual acuity on routine examination. The incidence of idiopathic chorioretinal folds among previously reported case series ranges from 14% to 17%.4-6 They are usually symmetric between eyes but can be unilateral, like in this patient. A diagnosis of idiopathic choroidal folds, as in this patient, can only be made once an etiology cannot be identified.The patient was seen 4 months later and his vision and refraction remained stable. Dilated fundus examination was notable for persistent choroidal folds.
Question: A healthy man in his mid-20s was referred to evaluate persistent, stable metamorphopsia in the left eye for 1 year. He had an enlarged blind spot in his left visual field for 2 years and denied symptoms of headaches, photophobia, blurred vision, or eye pain. Within the last year, the patient stated he had a 2-diopter hyperopic shift in his refraction of the left eye. He had undergone 2 magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) studies of his orbits over the previous year. Both were read as normal. His medical history was significant for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder and depression. He denied any previous ocular surgical procedures or trauma to either eye. He was prescribed bupropion.His visual acuity was 20/20 OD and 20/25 OS. His extraocular motility, pupils, and confrontation visual fields were normal. Intraocular pressures were 18 and 16 mm Hg in the right and left eyes, respectively. Anterior segment slitlamp examination findings were unremarkable in each eye. On ophthalmoscopic examination, the cup-disc ratio was 0.2, with both optic nerves appearing healthy with sharp margins. Retinal vessels were normal and the foveal reflex was sharp in each eye. The appearance of the macula in the left eye is shown in Figure 1.Optical coherence tomography with enhanced depth imaging (OCT-EDI) was normal in the right eye and showed undulations in the papillomacular bundle at the level of the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) in the left eye. Kinetic perimetry did not show enlargement of the blind spot or other visual field defect in each eye. What Would You Do Next?

caption: Clinical findings in 2020 showed complete clinical resolution. An atrophic scar on the earlobe and the infra-auricular area was seen.
explanation: A tissue culture was positive for Mycobacterium tuberculosis. The clinicohistologic findings and isolation of M tuberculosis confirmed the diagnosis of lupus vulgaris (LV). A trial of quadruple antituberculous therapy with isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol for 2 months followed by rifampicin and isoniazid for the next 7 months resulted in a rapid clinical response. The patient was then lost to follow-up for several years, until 2020, when current physical examination revealed complete clinical resolution (Figure 2).Clinical findings in 2020 showed complete clinical resolution. An atrophic scar on the earlobe and the infra-auricular area was seen.Lupus vulgaris is the most common type of cutaneous tuberculosis. It is a paucibacillary disease characterized by a chronic and progressive course that develops in a previously sensitized host with strong delayed hypersensitivity to tuberculin. In Western countries, LV is more common in females and predominantly occurs in the head and neck region, while in tropical countries, it is more common in males and predominantly involves the lower limbs.1 Lupus vulgaris may occur through hematogenous, lymphatic, or contiguous spread from a remote focus of disease elsewhere in the body. It may rarely follow primary inoculation or vaccination with bacille Calmette-Gu rin.2 Clinically, LV starts as an erythematous-brownish plaque that slowly expands over the years. A classic clinical sign is the apple jelly appearance on diascopy. Histologically, LV is characterized by a classic granulomatous tubercle with minimal caseation. The cellular inflammatory infiltrate includes epithelioid cells, Langhans giant cells, and mononuclear cells. Because bacilli are few and sparse in LV, acid-fast staining is rarely positive, and the yield of tissue cultures is also low.3-6 In such cases, polymerase chain reaction for tissue mycobacterial DNA may increase diagnostic accuracy.6 As patients with LV may have an active visceral focus of tuberculosis elsewhere, this should be sought out and excluded. To note, results of chest radiography are often normal, as the initial site of tuberculosis may be extrapulmonary.Originally, the term turkey ear was used to describe an enlarged, bluish-erythematous ear seen in lupus pernio.7 However, this presentation is not specific and may be seen in other skin diseases. Williams et al3 were, to our knowledge, the first to report LV with a turkey ear appearance and emphasized the need for a thorough clinical investigation in such cases. The chronic, relatively asymptomatic nature of LV may cause a significant delay in diagnosis. Indeed, the present patient s case is similar to the few previously reported cases that were diagnosed as LV decades after presentation.3-5The differential diagnosis of the present case includes inflammatory, infectious, and malignant conditions. Lupus pernio, a distinctive manifestation of cutaneous sarcoidosis, is characterized by violaceous papules and plaques, mostly affects adults, and is more common in Black women. The classic histopathological presentation of cutaneous sarcoidosis is the presence of  naked granulomas histiocytic granulomas devoid of prominent infiltrates of lymphocytes or plasma cells. Caseation is typically absent. Asteroid and Schaumann inclusion bodies may be seen within the multinucleated Langhans giant cells. Lupus pernio is often associated with systemic sarcoidal involvement. It is important to correctly differentiate between lupus pernio and LV because immunosuppressive treatment for the latter may have devastating consequences.Granulomatous mycosis fungoides is a rare subtype of mycosis fungoides characterized by infiltrates of atypical lymphocytes, histiocytes, and multinucleated giant cells.8 The diagnosis may be supported by immunohistochemistry and T-cell receptor gene rearrangement. Interestingly, a case of granulomatous mycosis fungoides of the auricle resembling a turkey ear that was initially suspected as LV has been described in the literature.9Leishmania recidivans develops in the site of previous infection that had healed and may mimic LV both clinically and histopathologically.10 Definitive diagnosis requires the demonstration of Leishmania-Donovan bodies or positive result of polymerase chain reaction testing. Other conditions that could be considered in the differential diagnosis of this case are granulomatous fungal infections, tuberculoid leprosy, and granulomatous syphilis.Although cutaneous tuberculosis has become rare in past decades, massive immigration from endemic countries may contribute to its reemergence in other regions of the world. Therefore, dermatologists worldwide should still keep a high index of suspicion regarding atypical cases of cutaneous tuberculosis as presented here.
Question: A woman in her 50s presented with an edematous, massively enlarged right auricle. The patient had immigrated to Israel 9 years before admission. She was adamant that the lesion had been present since early childhood but had gradually increased with a recent appearance of foul-smelling otorrhea. On examination, the auricle was markedly enlarged, edematous, and encircled by an erythematous, indurated plaque partially covered with a fine scale (Figure 1A). Diascopy revealed an  apple jelly  appearance. There was no local or distant lymphadenopathy; general physical examination findings were unremarkable.A, Clinical findings at presentation in 2008 showed an enlarged and edematous auricle encircled by an indurated erythematous plaque. Bloody otorrhea from the external auditory canal was seen. Image resolution was digitally enhanced. B, Biopsy specimen showed superficial and deep diffuse granulomatous dermatitis (hematoxylin-eosin). C, Higher magnification demonstrated classic tuberculoid granuloma with histiocytes and Langhans giant cells surrounded by lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate (hematoxylin-eosin).Results of routine laboratory investigations, including complete blood cell count, biochemical profile, liver function tests, and chest radiography, were normal. An intradermal Mantoux test induced a 20-mm induration. Histopathologic evaluation of the cutaneous lesion revealed noncaseating granulomatous dermatitis involving the dermis (Figure 1B). High magnification demonstrated tuberculoid dermal granulomas composed of histiocytes and Langhans giant cells surrounded by lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate (Figure 1C). Results of polarized light examination and periodic acid Schiff, Ziehl-Neelsen, Giemsa, Gram, and Grocott methenamine silver stains were negative for microorganisms. What Is Your Diagnosis?

explanation: Palisaded encapsulated neuroma (PEN), also known as solitary circumscribed neuroma (SCN), was originally reported in 1972 by Reed et al1 as a benign hyperplasia of axons and sheath cells derived from Schwann cell origin. Clinically, most PENs will present in middle-aged adults as an asymptomatic, slow growing, flesh-colored papule on the face or neck.1-3 However, rare cases have been reported on other sites including the oral mucosa, eyelid, and glans penis.4-6 In addition, there has been a singular report from 1998 of a PEN arising from the nasal mucosa.7 It is relatively rare for a patient to have a PEN protruding into the nasal cavity.Clinically, a PEN is often indistinguishable from a neurofibroma, fibrous papule, nonmelanoma skin cancer, or benign appendageal tumors.8 Thus, biopsy is often indicated, as in the case of this patient.
